headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: Good day, ladies and gentlemen, and welcome to the Tesla Inc. Fourth Quarter 2017 Financial Results Q&A Conference Call. [Operator Instructions] I would now like to turn the call over to Senior Director of Investor Relations, Mr. Martin Viecha. Please go ahead, sir.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext:
Thank you, Andrew, and good afternoon, everyone. Welcome to Tesla's Fourth Quarter 2017 Q&A Webcast. I'm joined today by Elon Musk, J.B. Straubel, Deepak Ahuja and Doug Field is on the line. 
Our 4Q results were announced at about 1 p.m. Pacific Time in the update letter we published at the same link as this webcast. During this call, we will discuss our business outlook and make forward-looking statements. These comments are based on our predictions and expectations as of today. Actual events or results could differ materially due to a number of risks and uncertainties, including those mentioned in our most recent filings with the SEC. [Operator Instructions] 
Before we jump to Q&A, Elon would like to have some opening remarks. Elon?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext:
Right, thank you. So 2017 was also a big year for Tesla. We launched the Model 3, which was our first mass production vehicle, and it's a huge step change for Tesla. A lot of challenges but I think we made tremendous progress on that front.
We also designed and installed and managed operation the world's largest battery in Australia, this largest battery by a significant margin. And that battery is exceeding its performance targets significantly. Also unveiled the Tesla Semi, which is a super heavy-duty truck maximum load semitruck and the next-gen Roadster, which we believe will exceed gasoline sports cars on every dimension. 
And we also achieved record production and deliveries of Model S and X. And overall, I think, while there were challenges associated with Model 3 ramp at the -- deep, we were in a deeper level than we expected. So a few levels deeper than we'd like to be but swiftly exiting, I think. And so really, I think, on balance a phenomenal year. And I'd like to thank everyone at Tesla, who should be very proud of the work they've done. This is incredibly difficult. I'd like to thank everyone for their hard work and contribution to 2017 being a really good year for Tesla.
I also want to thank our suppliers, particularly those involved in Model 3, as they've shared the vertical struggle we've had in ramping our production, and they've really but not all spent weekends and taking lot of risks and sat alongside us in the challenges associated with the [indiscernible] so I'd like to thank them for supporting us through this difficult time with Model 3.
As well, our customers and Model 3 reservation holders, you're going to love your cars and we are working to get them to you as quickly as we possibly can. As for Model 3 production, we continue to make significant progress every day, and we are targeting a weekly production rate of 2,500 vehicles by the end of March and 5,000 by the end of Q2. 
And as [indiscernible] you've seen in the letter, the quarter-over-quarter production of Model 3 is rising exponentially. So I hope we'll -- people think that if we can send a Roadster to the asteroid belt, we could probably solve Model 3 production. It's just a matter of time. So -- and really the on the timing are really quite small in the grand scheme of things. 
So it's -- 2018 is likely a very big year for us. At some point in 2018, we expect to begin generating positive quarterly operating income on a sustained basis, operating 5,000 a week of Model 3 production. And I'm cautiously optimistic that we will be GAAP profitable. It's not certain but it's -- I'm cautiously optimistic that we will actually be GAAP profitable with no asterisk.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Elon. Andrew, let's go to the first question.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: I'm showing we have a question from the line of Rod Lache with Deutsche Bank.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: Congratulations on the launch yesterday. Wanted to just ask a couple of questions. One is just we get a little bit more color from you on Model 3, what the production run rate is at the moment. Maybe if you can just provide us with a little bit more color on where the challenges are at this point? On the last call, you talked about, I think, 2 of the 4 zones at Gigafactory that were still kind of an issue in manual operation. Have those been resolved? And once you get to 2,500, is the ramp to 5,000, does that just nearly involve increasing line speeds?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: Sure. I'll try to give pretty as much color as possible. I'm reminded of, I think you may have been [indiscernible] about sausage, if you like, sausage and respect the [indiscernible] being made. And it's that is true of a production ramp. So I wouldn't read too much into the day-to-day battles of this or that, but I'll give you a color but don't like -- don't read too much into it. So yes, there are 4 zones in -- model production is fundamentally the limiting factor on Model 3 output, which is ironic since battery modules really should be the thing we're best at. And I think in part, we were probably a little overconfident, a little complacent in thinking about this is something we know and understand and put a lot of tension on other things, not -- and just got too comfortable with our ability to do battery modules. We've been doing that since the start of the company. And of the 4 zones, 2 of them, which are subcontracted to -- the production systems were subcontracted to other companies didn't work it turns out. We promised they would work and it just didn't work. So we had to do what would normally be maybe an 18-month development cycle for our production system of that scale and complexity and tried to do that in basically 6 months. Maybe a little -- 6 to 9 months. And we've talked about on multiple levels so we have a design that is nearing completion for a new automated system for zone 1 and 2. That is being led by a growing team. It's an excellent design. All the other support they've done is has performed just to spec. And we expect a single Tesla line to be equivalent to 3, if not 4, of the current lines that we have and be smaller, which is kind of amazing. And then we have what we call a semiautomatic line, which is a series of small automated stations manned by people. And they've actually been remarkably effective. It's actually restoring my faith in humanity that the rapid evolution of progress and the ability of people to adapt rapidly has -- is quite remarkable. Our semiautomatic sort of semi-manual semiautomatic line is exceeding all 3 of the automatic lines right now. So -- and that's something that we're able to scale quite rapidly. I mean, J.B., is there additional color you'd like to add on that?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: Sure, that's a great summary of it. I think much has been made about the manual production of modules. That's really not very accurate. These are, as Elon said, semi-auto lines where we have people moving materials perhaps between the machines that are actually performing operations. But there is still a degree of automation doing the operation.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: It's not artisanal.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: Exactly. And this is what has been ramping quite effectively in the last -- in the first half -- the first part of this year. So we're continuing to expand that, the semi-auto lines, and that is effectively bridging the gap as we redesign the full automation and bring that online.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: Yes. And it's pretty [indiscernible] So actually, I think it's probably worth providing some [indiscernible] for investors that are interested so you can actually see it first-hand. I think [indiscernible] is like if you see it first-hand, you will understand exactly what's going on. And so I think [indiscernible] for some investors that are interested because I think really you're going to really feel for what it is. Otherwise, it's just some that are kind of hard to put -- hard to imagine.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: I also just want to add, I think it's fair to say that this may be degree of complacency that happened at the end of last year has been pretty thoroughly replaced by an intense focus from a huge portion of the Tesla team. And there are a lot of different initiatives and teams, whole teams targeted at this area. So as Elon opened with, it's not a question of if we will get to the production rate, it's just a question of the matter of time.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: Well, it's probably a level of granularity that is not productive to dive into in terms of exactly what is coming from which operation. But we do expect the new automated lines to be landing and starting out at the Gigafactory in just the next landing in sight within this quarter.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: Yes, the new automated lines should arrive next month in March. And then it's already -- it's been -- it's working in Germany so that's going to be disassembled, brought out of the Gigafactory and reassembled and then into operation at the Gigafactory. It's not of question whether works or not, it's just a question of disassembly, transport and reassembly. So that's [indiscernible] -- yes. So we expect to alleviate that constraint. That -- we're alleviating that constraint. That's what gets us to the roughly 2,000 to 2,500 per week production rate. The next constraint would be material conveyance at our Fremont vehicle plant. And there's a very sophisticated automated parts conveyance. We think it's probably most sophisticated in the world, a matter of 1 that is more so. And the software of that is quite complex. So that would be the next constraint on production to get to 5,000 is the conveyance system in Fremont. So that also appears to be on track. So we feel like the around the unit volume predictions are getting some more with each passing week.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: And our next question comes from the line of Adam Jonas with Morgan Stanley.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: I also want to add my congrats for the launch yesterday, the Twin Falcon landing was probably the sickest thing I've ever seen in my life. First question's for Deepak. Given the negative trade cycle, your negative working cap, some of the modeling analysts are doing kind of simulating when you get to 2,500 or 5,000 or maybe somewhere in between that, that some of the arrangements you made with your suppliers who have been very helpful, that you might temporarily run enough negative working cap to even have operating cash flow exceed CapEx. Is that something that's possible? Or again, again I know it's execution behind that clearly but is that something out of question or barely even?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: I mean, we got to look at it from a full quarter perspective. The negative working cycle is amplified by the rate at which we ramp our production. Given our present lines of getting to 5,000 by end of Q2, it's a fairly gradual -- it's exponential from where we started but it's not going to create a situation where our cash flow from operations will exceed CapEx.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: And just as a follow-up, Elon, your kind of compensation -- long-term compensation plan obviously got a lot of attention and raised some questions, however long term from now on succession. Just wanted to ask, do you see your successor as CEO of Tesla someone currently within the company right now or from outside the company, kind of how do you see that?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: I mean, there's no actual search going on or there's not even -- or passive or active search going on for new CEOs on remain CEO for the foreseeable future. But at some point, if there's somebody really spectacular inside or outside the company who could take on that role and who are going to have that title in that role, that would be fine with me. And I would focus on product development, which is [indiscernible], which is what I like doing best. So -- but there are no plans to make change at the time.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018

componenttext: Well, there's a difference between 2 and 4 years. We have an initial exponential, which [indiscernible] exponential appears [indiscernible] extrapolate on a straight-line basis, exponential always predictions are conservative at the beginning. And then exponential takes off, and it becomes linear and then it becomes logarithmic. So it's easier to predict -- not far easier to predict the endpoints or the steady state of the escrow than anywhere on that exponential or log. So let's say, 4 years, I think 100,000 units a year is a reasonable expectation. Maybe more but that's the right -- roughly the right number, I think.

componenttext: Yes. I think we might be able to exceed the specs that we unveiled last year too, which is pretty exciting. speculation that we might not meet them, I think we're going to exceed them. So -- and I made this comment before. It's [indiscernible] last over these comments, but I really take these to heart. The competitive strength of Tesla long term is not going to be the car. It's going to be the factory. We're going to productize the factory. And really this is the lesson that is kind of obvious in history because the Model T wasn't a product. It was [indiscernible]. The Model T is a very simple car. Everybody could have made that car. But not anyone could make the [indiscernible]. And that's really what would be Tesla's long-term competitive advantage. But the factory is going to be the product that has the long-term sustained competitive advantage, in my opinion.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: And our next question comes from the line of Toni Sacconaghi with Bernstein.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Feb 07, 2018
componenttext: You commented in the shareholder letter that capital expenditures for 2018 were expected to be a bit higher than 2017. componenttext: Sure, I mean, our biggest -- a very high-level sort of breakdown. Our biggest investment is obviously in the Model 3. componenttext:
Thank you, Sherri, and good afternoon, everyone. Welcome to Tesla's First Quarter 2018 Q&A Webcast. I'm joined today by Elon Musk, J.B. Straubel, Deepak Ahuja and Doug Field.
Our Q1 results were announced at about 1 p.m. Pacific Time in the update letter we published at the same link as this webcast. Actual events or results could differ materially due to a number of risks and uncertainties, including those mentioned in our most recent filings with the SEC. [Operator Instructions] 
Before jumping into Q&A, Elon has some opening remarks. Elon?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2018 Earnings Call, May 02, 2018
componenttext:
Right, I think our letter says most of it. But I think we're going to spend extra time on Q&A, try to answer as many questions as possible. I think we should really answer [indiscernible] so we're going to go as long as best as there are good questions to answer. 
The thing I'm most excited about is the rapid increase in output. We got, just in the last [indiscernible] hours at Gigafactory managed to [indiscernible] of over 3,000 packs per day -- sorry, per week and actually reached a peak hour with -- if extrapolated, that would be right about 5,000 cars per week. And obviously, you can not take a peek hour and assume every peak -- every hour is as good as the peak. 
But it's both -- if you're going to achieve it, even once in an hour, then with continued refinement of the system and improved operational uptime of the machinery, it's -- you can achieve that sustained rate with more refinement. So we spent essentially a month or 2 improving the operational uptime and the system as a whole to be able to do well over 5,000, I think. 
I mean, what's interesting is that [indiscernible] the case of production we were able to do this with minimal CapEx. And I think in general, our understanding of production is improving dramatically, exponentially in fact. And we are seeing ways to achieve improved volume with dramatically less CapEx by simplifying production line, by really engaging our associates no matter how [indiscernible], improving the way that parts are made. 
It's amazing how everybody's got good ideas, it just makes [indiscernible] ideas and implement them. And then making ongoing design improvements so that when we [indiscernible] that, something that's not well designed for manufacturing that we're very quick to change that part design and introduce that into the flow.
[indiscernible] things that wasn't found is that there are some things that very well suited to manual operation, and some things are very well suited to automated operation. And the 2 should not be confused. So I should be clear that the vast majority of the Tesla production system is automated. However, as I mentioned in a tweet a few months ago, we did go too far in automation front and automated some pretty silly things. 
One example would be we have this sort of ironically foolish. We had these fiberglass mats on the top of the battery pack. But basically, like the [indiscernible]. So we tried to automate the placement and bonding of fluff to the top of the battery pack, which is ridiculous. So we had [indiscernible] which was really an incredibly difficult machine to make work. Machines are not good at picking up pieces of fluff, okay? Human hands are way better doing that.

That same -- one question I was asked do we actually need that so it doesn't occur with and without and found there was no change in the noise volume in the cabin. So we actually have part that was unnecessary that was forced line breaking down because [indiscernible] frequently just pick up the fluff. Put it in a random location. So that was one of the silliest things, I found.
We were also, in this [indiscernible] remains to be fixed but in a lot of cases but we're overgeneralizing the design. We've added costs, we've added manufacturing step, better failure mode and something that it is not necessary. So that is something [indiscernible]. That's changed.
So the result is we've had a radical improvement in production in battery pack virtually from taking 7 hours to make a pack 3 weeks ago to under [ 17 ] minutes now. So just really radical improvement [indiscernible] possible. 
We also saw improvement in production [indiscernible] point is clearly automated. So -- and we're able to also achieve a sustained rate of 3,000 [indiscernible] a week. So we're actually slightly ahead in battery module and pack production than expected. And with some work at the Fremont vehicle plant, primarily in the general assemble -- general assembly area, I'm confident we will very soon exceed the 3,000 mark in Fremont.
So already there in the body shop, which is also entirely automated where we [indiscernible] the body. Capable of over 3,000 [indiscernible] and the general assembly for some improvements, which will include reduction, I should say, temporary reduction in automation in a few places than we should be [ 3,000 ] able to unlock the So basically, I'm feeling really good about the Tesla production of Model 3. And I'm very proud of the work the team has done. It's been an amazing amount of hard work and sacrifice by some very talented people to achieve this outcome.
It's worth noting, the [indiscernible] charge in the Model 3 market share competitors and midsized premium sedans. We are almost the best-selling sedan in the United States in this category. And as of April, and we will certainly be the only unless something really odd -- I mean, [indiscernible] ran and really be [indiscernible]. 
In the third quarter, it will -- I think there's a good chance Model 3 gets maybe close to majority market share of midsized premium sedans, 40% -- 30%, 40% seems likely and maybe a majority market share later this year. This is coming from a standing SAAR against a lot of established brands who have far more sales than we do. So this is very encouraging.
Oh, and yes, as the letter says, I'm feeling quite confident about achieving GAAP net income and positive cash flow in Q3. This is not a certainty but it does appear quite likely in my view. During this call, we will discuss our business outlook and make forward-looking statements. These comments are based on our predictions and expectations as of today. Actual events or results could differ materially due to a number of risks and uncertainties, including those mentioned in our most recent filings with the SEC. [Operator Instructions]

componenttext:
Hi, thank you for joining. First of all, I'd like to say we're incredibly proud of the Tesla team for producing 7,000 Model 3, Model S and Model X vehicles in the last week of June. Those were amazing efforts. So honored to work with such great team to fuse that incredible result. It's like mind blowing. We continued to achieve 5,000 [indiscernible] per week, 7,000 combined S X and 3 [indiscernible] per week in July, showing that we were able to do this on a sustained basis. And we expect to, in the absence of force majeure or some very unexpected events, be able to achieve an average of 5,000 Model 3s or above for Q3 and 2,000 Model 3, Model S X or above per week for [ Q3 ] as well. So essentially 7,000 cars a week plus for an average for Q3. So it's an amazing jump from a probably year ago when we're producing 2,000 vehicles a week. So kind of a mind-blowing report for a manufacturing company. So yes, it's incredible work by the team to do that. Many, many late nights, weekends extreme effort and lots of smart ideas. It's amazing.
The results you're seeing is Model 3 market share has surpassed all competitor premium good-sized sedans and [indiscernible] So Model 3 market share is now a majority. July was a majority of all premium sedans. That trend is, we think, likely to continue. So it's not -- we do not think it will stop there. I have Robin Ren here, who's our worldwide Head of Sales to talk about some of the interesting [indiscernible] that we're seeing, a sense of cars that peoples are trading in, the sales and demand trends just looking really positive.
[indiscernible] great feedback from Model 3 from our customers, and we're now delivering the performance to motor and all of that versions. And the Model 3 reviews are outstanding, really couldn't ask for better viewers from some of the greatest critics of the world. And it's -- yes, and just the thing that we are [indiscernible] Model 3s we deliver to the field, it's actually closing  [indiscernible] overall growth of our sales. So we delivered our Model 3 to somebody, they love it, they tell all their friends. They are actually really -- our customers are our primary sales force. They love their car and take their friends for a drive, and that's the thing that fundamentally drives our sales. 
But not everyone has Model 3, obviously, so we need to get the cars out there for test drives as is right now. Not even [ both ] stores in North America have Model 3 for test drives. Of course, we prioritize cost of customers, but we are assuming it will have Model 3s available for test drives in all stores, and both the performance version and the four-wheel-drive version. So we just want people to [indiscernible] and test drive it, just not unreasonable. Although in Sunday, when I delivered a [indiscernible] testing out by direct delivery, it's definitely the future, direct delivery from factory to customers or wherever they are. The guy here who want it have never actually even sat in a Model 3. I'm like, wow, okay, how do you feel about the car now? You haven't driven anything else like it. It's amazing. So yes, it seems to be really well received.
Yes, so I'd approximate 7,000 cars a week. We believe we can be sustainably profitable from Q3 onwards. We will try to raise that [indiscernible] of Model 3 production steadily in the coming quarters and try to get to the 10,000 cars a week number as soon we can. What we've found as we spent a lot of time debarking wide range manufacturer issues is that the potential for our existing lines to be able to produce far more cars is much greater than expected that by simplifying production lines, by speeding them up, but in some cases being done manually instead of automatic and in other cases having done automatic instead of manual, we've been able to achieve dramatic improvements to existing lines. 
Our CapEx growing from 5,000 cars a week to 10,000 cars a week is a tiny fraction. CapEx of 5 to 10 is a tiny fraction of CapEx than if we go from 0 to 5,000 Model 3s. This is, I think, very good news for capital efficiency of the company. And with less [indiscernible] that's going to go from future mass market to vehicles that we produce. 
So from an operating standpoint, for Q3 [indiscernible] our goal is to be profitable and cash flow positive for every quarter going forward. Now obviously, there's a big recession or a severe force majeure event that [indiscernible] that's not always possible. But we're confident that provided the economy's roughly where it is today or reasonably good and has not big force majeure event that we -- I feel comfortable achieving the GAAP income positive and cash flow positive quarter every quarter from here now. It's -- I'll just say the major expectation, of course, we'll pay back a big loan or something where there may be -- just pay back a big loan. But absent that, we would be [indiscernible]
So once again, I thank the team for their incredible work and our customers for their support. Without the great people we have at Tesla and the customers who put their faith in us by buying our product, we will not be here today. And yes, I'm really more excited about the future of Tesla. We have super exciting sort of products to bring out in the future. And yes, [indiscernible] yes. Sorry if I sound a little tired. I've been working like crazy in the body shop lately. But it's really going great. Super excited. And again, some good people and a number of the executive team is here. [indiscernible] asked the [indiscernible] of the Tesla [indiscernible] team to be here. So [indiscernible] to go from here to see if our pilot leaders here at Tesla could introduce themselves and say a bit about what you're working on, what you're excited about in the future. headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Good day, ladies and gentlemen, and welcome to the Tesla Q3 2018 Financial Results and Q&A Webcast. [Operator Instructions] As a reminder, this conference is being recorded. I would now like to introduce your host for today's conference, Mr. Martin Viecha, Senior Director of Investor Relations. Mr. Viecha, you may begin.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext:
Thank you, Sherry, and good afternoon, everyone. Welcome to Tesla's third quarter 2018 Q&A webcast. I'm joined today by Elon Musk, J.B. Straubel, Deepak Ahuja and a number of other executives.
Our Q3 results were announced at about 1:00 p.m. Pacific Time in the update letter we published at the same link as this webcast.
During our call, we will discuss our business outlook and make forward-looking statements. These comments are based on our predictions and expectations as of today. Actual events or results could differ materially due to a number of risks and uncertainties, including those mentioned in our most recent filings with the SEC.
[Operator Instructions] But before we jump into Q&A, Elon has some opening remarks. Elon?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext:
Thanks, Martin. So I'll make some opening remarks and then we're going to talk about vehicle safety, Autopilot and factory safety. And we have a number of people from Tesla here to elaborate on that. So I think there's just a lot going on that you would find interesting.
But I want to start by thanking all of our customers, employees and shareholders. This was an incredibly stark quarter for Tesla. Model 3 production stabilized. We delivered a total of 84,000 vehicles globally, which is more than 80% of vehicles that we've delivered in all of 2017. In fact, we delivered more cars in this quarter than we did in all of 2016 in a single quarter.
Model 3 became the best-selling car in the U.S. in terms of revenue, and the third best-selling car in terms of volume. We saw higher revenues and significantly better profitability in our Energy business. I mean I think with solar, it may have been the best quarter ever for solar. We achieved GAAP net income of over $300 million, increased cash equivalents by $731 million and achieved a greater than 20% gross margin for Model 3. And moreover, we expect to again have a positive net income and cash flow in Q4. And I believe our aspirations will be something for all quarters going forward. I think we can actually be positive cash flow and profitable for all quarters going forward, leaving aside the quarters where we may need to do a significant repayment but -- for example, in Q1 next year. But I think even in Q1, I think we can be approximately flat in cash flow by end of quarter.
This quarter was made possible by the incredible execution of our employees across the board from sales, production, delivery, service, energy, engineering, finance and all of our G&A teams. Really every part of the business executing incredibly well. I want to thank everyone again for their incredible hard work. I especially want to thank customers who helped -- it's like I've never even heard of this -- happened before but I've never heard of a case where a company's customers actually cared about the future of the company so much that they volunteered their time to help the company succeed. I think that's amazing. Just -- you didn't see that anywhere.
So yes. Really -- actually. This quarter we start rolling out Version 9 of our software, which is the biggest software upgrade in 3 years. And Model 3 received a 5 star safety rating in every category and subcategory. I think that's probably a tough injury of any car that the U.S. government's ever tested.
Looking ahead, we expect to produce and sell even more Model 3s in Q4, and expect that trend to continue into Q1 and we're excited to bring Model 3 to Europe and China over next year, given that the market for midsized premium sedans in those regions is even larger than in North America.
I said before that we must prove that Tesla can be sustainably profitable. This quarter was an important step towards that, and I'm incredibly excited about what lies ahead. So this is -- yes, just so, so proud of the Tesla team, our customers. Really appreciate the support of our long-term shareholders. And yes, so on behalf of the Tesla team, we're just super appreciative of your support to what's actually been a very difficult time.
Right. Now let's move to -- let's start off with vehicle safety. Madan who's our lead vehicle safety engineer. Been with the company for a long time more than. Madan, how many years has it been we've been working together?

Yes. Thanks. Hi, everyone. I'm Andrej Karpathy, I'm the Director of AI here at Tesla. And my team trims all the neural networks that analyze the images streaming in from the all the cameras for the Autopilot. For example these neural networks identify cars, lane lines, traffic signs and so on. 
The team is incredibly excited about the upcoming upgrade for the Autopilot computer, which Pete talked about. This upgrade allows us to not just to run the current neural that works faster. But more importantly, it will allow us to deploy much larger computation, more expensive networks to the fleet. The reason this is important is that it is a common finding in the industry, and that we see this as well, is that you make networks bigger by adding more neurons, the accuracy of all their predictions increases with the added capacity. So in other words we are currently at a place where we've trained larger neural networks that work very well but we are not able to deploy them to the fleet due to computational constraints. So all of this will change with the next generation of the hardware, and it's a massive step improvement in the compute capability. And the team is incredibly excited to get these networks out there.

So there's a steady increase in the total supply that should keep us ahead of even Model 3 growth and also should let us have a larger percentage of energy supply be sourced from Giga locally.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Our next question comes from Pierre Ferragu with New Street Research.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: I was very surprised in the numbers you reported today that your gross margin performance on the Model 3. So if I remember correctly, you were expecting more or less a 15% type margin for this quarter, and you actually did better than 20%. So can you take us through what improved, like, faster and better than you had initially anticipated in the manufacturing line? And where these improvements came from?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Deepak here. And Jerome and others, please feel free to join. Our improvements on the cost side were in every aspect of cost. So clearly, our manufacturing labor hours improved significantly. Our overall manufacturing cost dropped almost 30% sequentially Q2 to Q3. We produced more volume so we had better fixed cost absorption. We had far less scrap. Our yield on each of the lines across both factories improved significantly. And as we look forward, we see even more opportunities. We are going through this phase where we are now stabilizing production and the teams can now intentionally focused on cost optimization. And that trend will just continue in Q4.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. I think we're also being roughly on the conservative side when we predicted. So we said 15% so we'll bring...

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Right. Our expectation was we would do better but we want to be conservative. You're right. And in terms of our guidance that we gave for Q3. Yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Okay. That's great. And then on -- as a quick follow-up, you've announced over the weekend like a mid-range -- with smaller battery packs. And I was wondering, as you're looking at expanding, for example, the Model 3, I think about it as you had 2 options. One was to go -- grow and to keep what you're seeing at higher end, higher ASB car. And the other 1 was to go for a lower cost car and stick to the U.S. So how did you decide the sequencing of these 2 things? Why is lower car now and going abroad in early next year?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Well, we're trying to provide the most affordable electric car options that we can. And since we can't -- we just don't have the ability to get to a $35,000 car right away. We thought this might be a way to offer it as an immediate step. And that's really it. Yes, we expect to start producing a significant volume for Europe in January. And obviously take some time to ship. So deliveries will probably pretty significant deliveries in Europe kind of in the late February, March time frame just because -- California to a customer in Europe. And for us, our car is only counted as delivered if it reaches the end customer and all the paperwork is completed correctly. So it's the highest possible standard for considering a sale a sale. Yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: And also the way that you start delivering cars.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. The -- we may or may not deliver cars in APAC in Q1 but certainly in Q2. It will be kind of borderline as whether cars delivered in APAC by the end of Q1. So I can't say it for certain. But definitely in Europe. But -- and then definitely in APAC in Q2. Yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Our next question comes from Romit Shah with Nomura Instinet.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: I guess just along those lines, Elon, you indicated that you're going to bring Model 3 to Europe early next year. Where would you like to see production in order to support that ramp overseas?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Well, initially, production will occur -- I mean, these last 6, several months, all production is -- vehicle production will take place in our car plant in California.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Sorry, I meant to ask where would you like to see the production rate on a weekly basis go to in order to support that ramp?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. It's hard to predict with accuracy. The -- and there's also like all the tariff laws and everything. So -- long term, -- say, look we're not talking about like next quarter. So like what is global -- likely global demand for a Model 3. It's probably in the order of anywhere from 500,000 to 1 million cars a year let's say quick global math Model 3. It's something like the 3 series, 1 -- around 0.5 million.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: 0.5 million

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: The BMW 3 series is about 0.5 million a year globally. And Germany we find that it's -- we outcompete the BMW 3 series quite well so it seems logical like that long term have a higher production or higher demand -- maybe somewhere between the kind of the BMW 3 series and the Volkswagen Golf which is about 1 million units a year. So yes, that's why I said anywhere from 500,000 to 1 million units a year, long term.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: And you can have to add new lines to support that? Or are you just continue to remove bottlenecks in the existing lines?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: No. We're just sticking to local production. In China, we're moving rapidly on that. So we're aiming to have Model 3 production for the China market for the greater China, market active certainly next year. It will be happening next year. But it will be done with a very capital-efficient manner. Much more akin to the way we did the general assembly line 4 versus general assembly line 3. And then we'll also have a factory in Europe long term because it's pretty silly to make cars in California and ship them all the way to Europe. That's far.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: First thing high logins.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. Exactly. Essentially it's -- but I'm not talking about S and X, I'm just talking about the 3. So S and X will continue to be made in California.  I think probably especially here. So for cars we're trying to maximize affordability, it makes a lot of sense to produce those cars at least in the continent where they are consumed or bought.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Our next question comes from George Galliers of Evercore.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Maybe just following up on the previous question. Is the target still to produce 10,000 Model 3s a week in 3 months? And I think you mentioned in the past that once you got to run rate of around 5,000, you'd be better placed to assess what CapEx is required to get there. So as of today, do you have a better idea of what CapEx is required to get to that kind of level at 3 months?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: I think we're not prepared to speak to that right now, except that it will be conservatively less than the money that we spent to get the 5,000 in the first. Like quite -- I think quite dramatically less. So like probably say half, like 7,000 units with Model 3 with really minimal CapEx.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Very minimal.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes, very minimal to get to 7,000 a week. And then that's really just basically solving -- improving the uptime of the existing lines. And we can the 7,000 a week. Yes, so -- and then it gets a little harder as you start to go above 7,000. We would need to at least bring lines down in Fremont for significant upgrades to get to 10k. But also just not -- we're not talking about massive amount of CapEx. But say like long term it's -- again long term it's, -- predicting these on a quarter-by-quarter basis is very difficult because when you have an exponential growth rate like we do -- I mean if look at Tesla's cumulative deliveries over time, it's like the cleanest exponential curve that I've ever seen. So -- but small movements in calendar time can look like a very large hit or miss one way or the other, because it's such a steep curve. That's why I'm -- it's very tricky to predict things on a quarterly basis. But a lot easier if you go out a year or so. Yes. I mean, it -- probably, long term it's at least sort of 7,000 to 10,000 cars from Fremont of Model 3. And then I don't know, 5,000 to 8,000 in the rest of world something like that. It's just a guess.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Okay. And then just a follow up, in the letter, you do point out the size of the European market for premium midsized sedans is roughly twice that of the U.S. Could you also just maybe comment to what your expectations are for mix in Europe. Based off Model S and Model X., do you expect a richer mix in Europe versus the U.S. or is it fairly similar?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: We've given that 0 thought. I mean, this is like -- there's not -- like -- I don't know, Martin, do you have anything to add?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. All I'm aware of is that -- because of cold weather and probably all-wheel drive and long-battery range will be highly demanded in Europe. But apart from that -- I mean, we ultimately have to start selling the car to see what the demand is. So...

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. I mean, it seems like it's likely to be comparable to -- I'd mean it really much market is like twice big in Europe than -- will slightly be at least as much demand in Europe as is normal -- that's what we can say about that. But our goal really is to make electric cars that everyone can afford, not just sort of [ 9 ] -- high option value cars. It's like we could produce a $35,000 car today, we would do it. We need more work. There's more work to do before we can make a $35,000 car and have a be positive gross margin. I'm not -- we're probably less than 6 months from that but that's our mission.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: [Operator Instructions] Our next question comes from Maynard Um with Macquarie.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: As you continue to scale the business, can you talk about how we should think about how you balance profits versus reinvestments? You're targeting sustainable GAAP profitability and cash flow. But I'm curious if there's a level of GAAP profitability or GAAP operating margin or flow you want to hold and then take the excess to fund new growth or accelerate opportunities?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Sure. I mean, maybe if I characterize that question it'll be like -- are we starving new vehicle development in order to achieve GAAP profitably and cash flow positive? Would that be an accurate -- is that essentially -- like the answer is no. So we've made significant progress on the Model Y. So in fact I approved the prototype to go to production recently. So it will be 2020 before that's in volume production. But we made great progress there. Also continue to make progress on the semi and the newer Tesla Roadster. And then actually the product I'm most excited about is the Tesla pickup truck. It's like I think it's going to be some next level stuff there. And then I should not -- forget to mention the solar tower roof will also start going into volume production of the solar tile roof next year. That's quite a long development cycle for -- because anything that's a roof is going to last 30 years. So even if you accelerate life testing as far as possible, there's still amount of time to do that and there's a lot of engineering that goes into how do you put on the solar tile roof with a -- and not be really labor-intensive in doing so. So there's a lot of engineering in the tile but in the way it's done. And then we've got -- continued improvements in Powerwall, Powerpack, other energy products. I think we've got the most exciting product road map of any company by far. And I'm not even sure like  probably twice I don't even know who would have -- which company would have a better product road map or even close. Yes. Maybe I don't know about them.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Great. And when you think about Tesla having its own ride sharing fleet or giving people the ability to loan out their car like an Airbnb model, I'm curious if your long-term plan is to build a platform that's going to enable companies to write applications to turn the car directly into an application? And then can you also maybe talk about the business model. Is that -- should we be thinking about like a revenue-sharing model sort of like how Apple takes a piece of revenue generated from applications from iPhones?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: I don't know about turning the car into an application, exactly. But I mean, maybe. But what we're trying to do things that maximizes usefulness. And so if there's a way we're thinking about where third parties could do something, then that could make sense. But I do know for sure that Tesla will operate its own ride hailing, right -- its own ride hailing service will compete directly with Uber and Lyft obviously. And then also have the ability for customers to offer their car and add or subtract their car to the fleet at will. It will be a company-owned fleet. And the company-owned fleet will just be where there aren't enough customer cars to be lent out. So if we find like in a particular metro, there aren't enough customers who are willing to add their car to the shared fleet, then that's where we'll stuff them with a Tesla-owned fleet. So that's why it's sort of combination of Uber-Lyft thing and Airbnb. And then we'll charge something probably comparable to the app to help us -- the app store works or I don't know we charge [ 30% ] or something in order for somebody to add the car to the fleet. I think that's like a pretty sensible way to go. Yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Our next question comes from Adam Jonas with Morgan Stanley.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Actually, I was going to restrict questions to operational topics. [indiscernible]

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: No problem. Yes, I do. Can you tell us about the folks who are taking deliveries of Model 3? What are the top cars, car models or brands that they're trading in or switching out of? How many are new to the brand? Anything you're prepared to share and then I've got a follow up.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Sure. Absolutely.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Hey, this is Martin. So I've done all of the analysis of all the trade-ins that we've received. And really the only pattern that I've seen is that it's sort of all across the board, and the vast majority is non-premium brands. I think that is the #1 message is just that more than half of the trade-ins we received were priced at below $35,000 when new. But other than that, there's no real pattern. I haven't noticed anything worth highlighting other than it's just a lot of people upgrading their cars quite dramatically.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: This is a huge upgrade. Yes, for most people -- well, for us but for many people, it is the most expensive car they've ever bought. So they're clearly demonstrating worth their money that they're willing to spend extra money to get a Tesla. So like suddenly in a sense like mass-market premium.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: And the price walk is way beyond the federal tax credit. So clearly there's value to the car that they are receiving, whether it's cost of ownership, whether it's sustainability, whether it's the brand or the product itself.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: And the safety.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: And the safety. All of the above is making a lot large number of customers jump up significantly in their purchase price.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. I mean really I think -- I honestly feel like the top reason to refer a friend to buy a Tesla is it's going to keep your friend safe.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: That's a good reason. If I can just squeeze in since I couldn't ask the first one that you could answer, do you think the third quarter is a milestone, Elon, where you think Tesla becomes sustainably self-funding and perhaps not in need of outside capital?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. That is our goal. We do not intend to raise equity or debt. If -- our intention right now that may change in the future. But the current operating plan is to pay up our debts, not to refinance them but to pay them off and reduce the debt load and overall leverage of the company. So actually I've forgot 1 quite important thing. As -- and this is life helpful why sort of crisis situations with logistics, for example. I would like again to  the inventory like basically finished product inventory from factory to the customer, I was quite surprised to see how long that took, and that it was part quite expensive in a lot of cases to get cars to customers. This was something I didn't fully appreciate before. And we really have a major initiative at Tesla to get the average time from a car exiting the factory to receiving the check from the customer, being in the customers hands if we can only get the check when we give the car to the customer. So getting car from factory to customer to get that to be as short as possible. In August, the average time in North America to get a car from the factories to the customer was 30 days, which is embarrassingly long. By the end of the quarter, we've reduced it to around 20 days. And our goal in Q4, this is a goal not a promise, but our goal is to get the average time for the car from the factory to a customer under 10 days. This is a giant improvement in the capital efficiency of the company. Because we're making on the order of $75 million worth of products per day -- of cars per day. So every day, it requires $75,000 -- $75 million worth of capital. So every 10 days, it's $750 million. And we -- obviously we have a loan from the bank that we can make use of. But the banks will only loaned us 85% of the cost of the vehicle, which translates to about 70% of the price of the vehicle. So -- and then we've got this loan outstanding which effectively increases the COGS of the car. And it dilutes the company to a tune of 30% of one of the inventory -- of the finished goods in transit is. So this is really, like tightening then getting that below 10 days in North America and then also improving dramatically the time of the transit time to Europe and Asia where we have local factories, it's actually very important for capital efficiency of the overall system. Because I think over time you want to get the time from a car going from factory to customer under 7 days worldwide. And then the terms that we have from our suppliers, on average, just over 60 days. Now our products inventory management also there's a lot of room for improvement there. We think we probably kept that down to a few hundred million dollars or so, Deepak, something like that, maybe $200 million to $300 million of COGS at the factory. So that effectively, what we're going to do is reverse the working capital requirement for the company quite dramatically so to a point where the faster we grow, the more capital we have. This is incredibly important for capital efficiency of the company. It's night and day. Deepak, is there anything you'd like to...

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: No, I think you are totally -- we are reducing the raw material inventory on one hand by keeping production stable, finding efficiencies and warehouse management and supply chain. And at the same time, reducing the time to deliver the car and we convert that car into a cash. And that significantly improves working capital needs.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. It's really quite dramatic. So -- yes, I think they're sort of profiling changes the financial effectiveness of Tesla.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. We have reduced our inventory in Q3, which helped. Although we had higher payables because -- sorry, higher receivables because the quarter ended on a weekend. We won't have that in Q4. So all of this should continue to help us in Q4 and beyond, the working capital game.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. And I mean of course, that's -- even if the only thing -- like even if this was the only thing that Tesla does different was to shorten the time from factory to the end customer -- any given company that did that will outcompete all other companies over time. It will not be a contest.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Our next question comes from Toni Sacconaghi with Bernstein.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: I have one for Deepak and then a follow up, please. Deepak, the OpEx expense management was very strong in the quarter. I think it was down 13% sequentially and OpEx was only up 5% year-over-year despite revenue growing 71%. So on that front, I mean, in hindsight, did you get too bloated and needed to get more rightsized? And looking forward, how do we think about OpEx growth versus revenue growth on kind of a more normalized basis?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. Tony, so excluding one-time items, our OpEx decreased sequentially by 5%, to just clarify that first of all. And a lot of that was driven by the actions we took in Q2 to be more efficient but with our employee headcount. We benefited from that in Q3. And we were really careful in terms of all of our spending. The other piece that helped us in terms of our a lot of our Model 3 spending on expense sort of R&D is reducing because Model 3's going into production, so Q2 to Q3, we saw a reduction there. And it just gives you a sense of the leverage of our operating expenses can have while our revenue was growing dramatically. So our OpEx will increase in the future but in a far slower rate and we will continue to be really, really careful about the spending. And I think there are actually more efficiencies than we can find.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: We are going to find them, absolutely.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Right. So we'll continue down that path.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Definitely.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: And then to follow-up, I was just wondering if you can help us a little bit on the -- back to the gross margin on Model 3 and the $35,000 car. So this quarter, I impute that Model 3 ASPs where maybe $59,000. And that might suggest that gross margins on a $35,000 Model 3 might be about 0. And Elon, I think you alluded to the fact that the goal is really to get positive gross margins on a $35,000 car before shipping. Are those all fair assessments? And I guess the question is where is -- where would a Model 3 -- $35,000 Model 3 be in terms of gross margins today? And where does it need to be before you want to offer it broadly to consumers?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. I mean, it's -- look the -- for asking question of that nature in detail is that it is a rapidly changing situations. So like literally, if you would ask this in a month, it'll be different. In another month, it would be different. The -- there's no question we need to get to a point where we can sell a $35,000 car and where the full account of the -- car is, let's say, on the order of $30,000 or slightly less than $30,000. So I think we want to ideally get the COGS of the car -- the car under $30,000. That would be -- that's our goal. That's what we're pushing very hard to achieve.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Exactly. And it's just a matter of time. It's a significant material cost reduction that comes there's battery types, fewer model cells. It's not the same cells that we have in the existing cars. [indiscernible]

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: And it's the same amount of [indiscernible]

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: It's the same amount of service. So same costs.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Fewer cells. And then the non-cell portion of the pack is also cost reduced. With the current mid-range pack, it still has the same -- basically about the same non-cell portion of the pack cost.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Exactly. And we are seeing massive reduction in all our manufacturing cost per car, which will continue. And as volume grows, that also helps us the fixed cost absorption. So it's the same factors that have helped us so far. Will continue to help us moving forward to get us there. Anything you want to add, J.B.? No?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Your next question comes from James Albertine with Consumer Edge.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Wanted to -- just a point of clarification. Elon, you mentioned in August the time to get the car from a factory to customer was 30 days, down to 20 at the end of the quarter, and your goal's under 10 by the end of 4Q. Where do we see that flow through from a COGS perspective? Is that in automotive gross margin? Or is that in services and other at this point?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. it's all in automotive gross margin. Our logistics cost, outbound [indiscernible] COGS. Automotive.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Outbound logistics. Yes, I think we'll see a reduction in inbound logistics as well as outbound logistics. Maybe a question like for the debt that is carried for that period, is that factored into COGS or is that not?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: The interest expense of the debt, that's in the interest expense line, okay, not in COGS.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Okay, yes. That's why I don't think like -- the difference COGS should probably -- to include anything that's directly driven by volume. Essentially that affects the marginal cost of the vehicle. So although that is not in -- officially in COGS, in my opinion, it probably should be to take the ABL interest expense and apply that effectively to the cost of the car.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: And from a broader sense, you're looking at it as the cost of doing business which can be awarded.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes. Just the cash flow money increases quite dramatically. Dilution or leverage outside of the ABL line improves dramatically. And then the fact of the cost -- the effect of cost to the car also reduces because you don't have the interest expense. You don't have Interest expense over 20 days versus 10 days is a big difference.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018
componenttext: Understood. I appreciate that clarification. Sort of what I was trying to get at, you've been running a negative gross margin in services and other for several quarters now. And wanted to get a sense for when that could maybe trough and start to turn the corner and to generate some profit for you. I understand there's a lot of building out going on for sales, service and charging infrastructure. But if you give us some kind of clarification there, that would be, I think, helpful. And if you're willing to maybe provide an update on where you stand today in terms of battery costs. headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Oct 24, 2018

Like we really care about providing the end customer with the most affordable car that we possibly produce, the best viability. And if we could do this pack now, we would absolutely would. It's going to take us, I don't know, at least 3 months to get the production going in the U.S. floor production. And that production's going to go to -- we're going to make packs. Packs going to go to vehicle factory. We've got the cars that we're going to to get delivered to customers. And one thing -- you do try to get kind of points that are worth bearing in mind. So if we've got the best in terms of miles superior per kilowatt hour and we also have the lowest cost per kilowatt hour. This makes it very difficult for other companies to compete with Tesla because we're the most efficient car and the lowest cost batteries. So I'd encourage our competitors to really make a huge investment. Now we've been saying that for a long time. We have -- they can use our Supercharger network if they can just have an adapter for our connector or something. We want to be as helpful as possible to the rest of the industry. The fact of the matter is we made the investment in the Gigafactory and the other companies didn't. And we put a lot of effort into having extremely efficient cars, which having the most efficient powertrains and the other companies didn't. But should or will over time. But that's what put us in quite a strong competitive advantage -- competitive position right now.
