headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Good day, ladies and gentlemen, and welcome to the Tesla, Inc. Q4 2018 Financial Results and Q&A Webcast. [Operator Instructions] As a reminder, this conference is being recorded. I would like to introduce your host for today's call, Mr. Martin Viecha, Senior Director of Investor Relations. Mr. Viecha, you may begin.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext:
Thank you, Sherry, and good afternoon, everyone. Welcome to Tesla's fourth quarter 2018 Q&A webcast. I'm joined today by Elon Musk, J.B. Straubel, Deepak Ahuja and a number of other executives. And our Q4 results were announced at about 1 p.m. Pacific time in the update letter we published at the same link as this webcast. 
During this call, we will discuss our business outlook and make forward-looking statements. These comments are based on our predictions and expectations as of today. Actual events or results could differ materially due to a number of risks and uncertainties, including those mentioned in our most recent filings with the SEC. [Operator Instructions] But before we jump into Q&A, Elon has some opening remarks. Elon?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext:
Thanks, Martin. Last year was definitely the most challenging year in Tesla history, but also the most successful. Thanks to the incredible work of the Tesla team, Model 3 became the best-selling premium vehicle in the U.S. for 2018. And in fact, when considering battery electric vehicles, Tesla achieved an 80% market share of U.S. sales in the last year. I think this point is perhaps not well appreciated. All other electric vehicles combined were 20% of sales in the U.S. in last year. So I think that's not so bad.
We also delivered -- also made electric vehicles last year as we did in all prior years combined, which is [indiscernible] by the Tesla team. If you tracked our Tesla vehicle production year-over-year cumulative sales deliveries year-over-year, it is about the cleanest exponential I've ever seen. We've basically almost doubled our fleet every year. Every year, we make as many cars as we did in all prior years. This is a very unusual thing to see for, especially for a large complex manufactured object. I think maybe the fastest that a complex manufactured object like a car has grown in history or at least I'm not aware of anything that is faster. Martin, are you?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: I'm not sure. I think Model T was a little bit slower but I'm not 100% sure.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext:
Okay. And we expect that exponential to continue so with the deliveries this year being -- even in the face of -- if there's a global recession, even if there's a global recession, we're expecting deliveries this year to be about 50% higher than last year. And this could be a lot more than that. But even with tough economic times, to receive 50% growth is pretty [ nutty]. 
For Q4, we achieved GAAP profitability -- the second quarter for the first time in the company history, and we increased our cash on hand by more than $700 million even after paying debt in EMEA with the total of $3.7 billion of cash. This means we have enough cash to settle our convertible bond that will mature in March. In addition, our operating margin remains strong at 5.7%. Operating margins in the fourth quarter are usually lower than in our history, but this was not the case for Tesla.
2019 is going to be an amazing year for Tesla. As I mentioned, we're expecting to increase sales by 50%. Perhaps could be a lot more than 50%, but I think 50% is a very reasonable number, but that's crazy growth for the automotive industry. 
I will note that one of our major priorities this quarter is improving service operations. So really, from my standpoint, when I think about some of what my priorities are this quarter, it's improving service in North America. That's #1. And I think very exciting we're going to roll out new Tesla commercial App. We're going to get cars to China and Europe and make sure that we have good logistics for the whole delivery process from factory gate to the customer. That sounds like pretty far from California to get to Europe and China and make it to a good bunch of our customers, so we're working every aspect of that logistics chain. And that I think we've -- I think it's going to be good. I'd say at this point, I'm optimistic about being profitable in Q1. Not by a lot, but I'm optimistic about being profitable in Q1 and for all quarters going forward. 
So let's see, we've opened 27 new store and service locations, bringing out our total locations worldwide to 378, and we increased our Mobile Service fleet to 411 vehicles. Mobile Service fleet is something we can scale up very rapidly because we don't need to break some water, we can get more vehicles, hire people and deploy rapidly. It actually results from higher customer satisfaction because we can actually send one of our service bands to your work or home and fix the car without you having to bring it into the service center or missing work or anything like that. It's really seamless and visible and customers love it.
We also increase the functionality of the Tesla App service so that instead of having to make an appointment to call and make an appointment, you just open your Tesla App and say you want to make a service appointment, and top 10 most frequently requested service items, and with a couple of taps, you made yourself an appointment. And we're going to make it easier for the car to be picked up and dropped off as well. So if you prefer not coming to the service center at all, you can just request that the car be picked up and delivered. That's something that -- so that's already -- we're going to roll that and have big improvement to customer satisfaction. They rolled it out 2 or 3 weeks ago. But the next thing we're going to add is, if a car detects something wrong, like a flat tire or a drive unit failure, that before the car has even come to a halt, there is a tow truck and a service on the way. So the car has already notified Tesla emergency services and a service letter, a tow truck are on their way before your cars even come to a stop. Now this will be immense in driving customer happiness.
Virtually, you just call it, tap the ring to cancel it. So you can cancel it if you want. We accept it automatically going to happen. You just press cancel. 
We're also improving product distribution. So we made a strategic error in the past about not having service parts located at our service centers. We had them in product distribution warehouses which basically meant it was possible to have a fast turnaround on servicing a car because the car would come in, then the parts have to requested, they come to the service center, it would basically -- even if very simple, the part could take days. So we're going to be able to be stock in all common part at the service centers so that it's possible to and will have get your car serviced in 20 minutes or 15 minutes even if it's a simple matter. In like 8 minutes or whatever, [indiscernible]. Just lightning fast. But in order to have that, we have to have the parts located in our service centers. Also, it's going to make sense for our service centers to do basic priority work or essentially replaced [indiscernible] a front or rear [indiscernible]. It makes sense to just prestock the front and in common colors. So unless you have an unusual color which will replace your [indiscernible] in 20 minutes, and this is not a spec [indiscernible] weak body shop stuff.
So in terms of new products, with Model Y, we've completed ensuring the Model Y and the products are going up for production Model Y. 3 quarters of the Model Y is common with the Model 3, so it's much lower CapEx per vehicle than Model 3, and the rest is also quite low. This is in contrast to Model S versus Model X where the theory was -- I think Model X, we just -- it's Model X to be like the sort of the Faberge egg of cars. It's an incredible vehicle. Probably one -- probably nothing like it will ever be made again, and maybe it shouldn't. But it is a work of art, it's a special work of art, but the commonality with the Model S is limited. There's only about maybe 30% in common with the Model X, whereas Model Y is, I think, 76% has got in common with the Model 3. And we're most likely going to put Model Y production right next to -- in fact, it's part of our main Gigafactory in Nevada. So it will just be right there, batteries and powertrains will come up and goes right into the vehicle. So that also reduces our risk of execution and reduces the cost of having transfer parts from California to Nevada. It's not a for sure thing but it's quite likely, and it's our default plan.
I would expect Model Y will probably be, the Model Y will be maybe 50% higher than Model 3, could be even double. The -- as I understand it, the midsized SUV segment is the -- worldwide is the most popular type of vehicle, so we'll probably see a higher volume of Y than 3. 
And earlier this month, we saw the construction of our Gigafactory in Shanghai, and by the end of this year, we expect to be producing Model 3s using a complete vehicle production line. That's body paint, final assembly, general assembly and lateral production. So it basically would be extremely fast. I get daily updates of progress of the Shanghai Gigafactory, and those factories going to go up like lightning. So we do feel quite confident at this point, at least for the factories that are in our control, that we can achieve volume production in Shanghai by the end of the year. And that should allow us to get to the 10,000 vehicles a week rate or very close to it, I think, at the end of the year. And yes, I think that's it.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Okay, great. So we're going to take the first questions from our retail investors who have been submitting their questions on say.com. So the question that has been submitted has been about service, which I think you already spoke at length about. So let's go to the second question.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: The second question would be, how are you feeling about demand right now across the product line? Is 500,000 to 700,000 units at 42,000 ASP still a realistic annual target for Model 3 even considering Model Y and its impact on demand? And do you continue to see S and X the amount of 100,000 annually?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: My best guess, this is just a guess, my best guess for demand of Model 3 worldwide is something, in a strong economy, it's something in the order of 700,000 or 800,000 units a year. That's my best guess for demand of Model 3 in a strong economy. If the economy goes into a recession, then I think that could be something [ under ] 40% less. But I think even in a recession, worldwide demand is still something in the order of 500,000 for Model 3. For S and X, we did eliminate the 75 [indiscernible] of S and X and product model differentiation relative to 3 and then Y that's coming out. I think we could see a slight decline in total vehicles, but I think the net cash flow from S and X is likely to be very similar. So probably no major change in net cash flow for S and X.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Okay. The next question from Alex is, can you please share an update on [indiscernible] of driving test and Tesla network development? When will customers start to see full of driving features? What's the best case down the line for a Tesla network to go live?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Sure. So we have -- we already have full Self-Driving capability on Highway. Highway on ramp to Highway exit, including passing cars and highway interchanged to another, full Self-Driving capability is there. In a few weeks, we'll be pushing update that will allow the option of removing stoke confirm in markets where regulators approve it, which we believe that will be in the case in the U.S., for example. And over time probably within all regulators will approve it. But we kept talking from there just to make sure that we took care of like any [indiscernible] cases. It's really quite sublime if you have stock and the cars go from higher on ramp, passes slower cars, it changes -- takes an interchange and then takes the exit and then comes to a stop after the exit. So it's really quite profound to have that experience. Then the next part of full Self-Driving will really be to -- is traffic lights, it's hard. Stoplights are pretty easy because we've got the geocoded those and is recognized at all times. Traffic lights and intersections will be the next really tricky one and then navigating complex parking lots and like sort of like a underground in a mall parking lot with lighter traffic and pedestrians and it's on multiple levels, that kind of thing is what gets tricky. With the release of enhanced or advanced sonar, you'll see the first indications of the car being able to navigate complex parking lots, and that's also coming up fairly soon, probably next month. And in development mode, the car does all things I just mentioned in development mode. It recognizes traffic lights and stop signs, and these guys, all the functionality is about [indiscernible] it's really a question of getting your liability of working in traffic lights to several names. So I guess like 98% good right now, but we need it to be like 99.999% really, really extremely reliable. So and the capability will be there for -- when will we think it's safe for full Self-Driving? It's probably towards the end of this year, and then it's up to regulators to decide when they want to approve that.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Okay. Let's go to the next question, which is, if and when will Tesla switch Model S and X to 2170 battery cells? What percent range improvement do you expect?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: We have no plans to which S and X to 2170, and I can't comment on huge product developments.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Okay. So maybe we'll take the last question from retail investors, which was, where will Tesla Semi and Model Y be produced? Can you share timeline on expected production ramp of these products?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: As I mentioned earlier, Model Y, we think, most likely will be produced at Gigafactory, but that's -- unless we encounter some obstacle. That's the default plan that we're proceeding towards. And it's fast, low risk and also relatively low CapEx. In terms of the -- I mean, probably there's like in our initial production of Model Y in very low volume, probably next year, but then it always takes time to ramp up any production system, and that's difficult to predict the shape of that S-curve. So we feel confident in saying there will be production volume Model Y by the end of next year, but in between beginning of next year with low volume, very low growth exponentially -- from beginning of last year and end of next year, we will break that ramp. So that's actually our expectation for Y. For Semi, we're -- I don't know if I can make a comment on that.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: You want to stock next year as well?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: But the first units will be your own. First units will be for own usage. So depends how many trucks we'll use for own usage to move the parts in the vehicle different location, and then we'll start delivering to outside customers.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, that's good. And then the Tesla pickup truck, we might be ready to unveil that this summer. There will be something quite unique, unlike anything else.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Okay. Fantastic. So operator, we can start taking questions from participants on the call.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: [Operator Instructions] Our first question comes from Ryan Brinkman with JPMorgan.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: I've seen the amount that you've spent on lands for Gigafactory in Shanghai in the classification operating cash flows. Is there any guidance you can provide us in terms of how to think about CapEx for this facility going forward? And can you just discuss source of funds for the project? I think you've spoken in the past about the potential terrains that locally in China? Is that still your thinking? And what kind of terms might you be able to raise that capital?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, Deepak here. You're right. The purchase of the land is a 50-year lease with the government of China, so it's not CapEx, but it's operating lease, and that shows up in cash flow from operations. However, the CapEx that we will invest is our equipment, and we fully own it, so that will show up as capital expenditures. The plan, as we have indicated in the letter, is still to get funding for majority of that capital spending from local China banks. And we expect pretty attractive rates based on the dialogue we've had in this lower interest, and we hope to finalize that and then share the details at that point.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes. I mean, as a ballpark figure, probably it's something about -- something in the order of $0.5 billion in CapEx to get to the 3,000 vehicle rates in Shanghai, ballpark figure. And as Deepak was saying, very competitive debt financing in China, really extremely compelling interest rates, and we do not expect that to be a capital drain on the company.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, these are the biggest banks in the world, and for them, $400 million is not a large amount of money on the scheme of things.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Our next question comes from Gene Munster with Luke Ventures.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Sorry, perhaps, if you're an ordinary [indiscernible] maybe not as obvious to everyone is Tesla has the first wholly-owned manufacturing in China of any automotive company. This is profound, and we're very appreciative of the Chinese government allowing us to do this. I think it is symbolic of the open the market, and if everyone, a note of appreciation for the Chinese government allowing us to do that. It's a very significant thing.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: The question I had is related to Waymo and the autonomous driving opportunity. Morgan Stanley recently valued Waymo at $175 billion. And my question is, what do they have that you don't have? And separately, so what do they have that you don't have? And then separately, how important is the timing in the Tesla story longer term that this may really about EVs and renewable energy? Or is the autonomy kind of one of the foundational parts of the story longer term?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: [indiscernible] Tesla, yes, so in fact, the Y at Tesla, the relevance, what's the point of Tesla comes down to 2 things: Acceleration of sustainable energy; and autonomy. Sustainable energy is absolutely fundamental because this is the next potential risk for humanity. So that is, by far and away, the most important thing. And also, very important is autonomy, the potential to save millions of lives, tens of millions of serious public injuries and give people their time back. So they don't have to drive, if you're on the road, you can spend time doing things that you enjoy instead of being in terrible traffic. So it's extremely important. We feel confident about our technical strategy, and I think we have an advantage that no one else has which is that we have this, at this point, somewhere in the order of 300,000 vehicles on the road with 360 viewing camera sensors with radar, ultrasonics, always connected uploads, and especially pretty equipped with customer summation witness intervention. So effectively, we have a massive, massive training fleet. So the amount of training that we have, if you and everyone else up combined, they're probably 5%, I'm being generous -- of the miles that Tesla has. And this difference is increasing. A year from now, we'll probably go -- assuming 18 months from now, we'll probably have 1 million vehicles on the road with -- and every time the customers drive a car, they're training the systems to be better. I'm just not sure how anyone competes with that.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: This is Jerome. Well, our manufacturing is improving quarter over quarter, actually week over week. We take fewer hours, both at here in Fremont or at the Gigafactory, to assemble the Model 3 and S and X as well. And then we track the quality very closely. We review that carefully with the engineers and the supply chain and manufacturing chains. And the quality in the field and the number of incidents is also improving week over week, every week, so there are fewer and fewer need for cars to be in service, yes. So we'll keep going there when there's no end in sight. We'll try to make sure that the car never breaks down.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Just a follow up on the comments, [indiscernible], you said about 700,000 to 800,000, you think, is normal demand. Any color on what price you're expecting that to be? Because I think there's a lot of chatter the demand is already weak in -- or at the midrange at least already in January, and I wonder if that's true as well.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes. I mean, there are multiple factors at play here. First of all, there's a lot of seasonality to automotive purchases. First of all, you do not buy a new car in the middle of a blizzard. So January and February tends to be seasonally low and then picks up significantly around the early to mid March timeframe. In the U.S., growth had a pull forward of demand from the tax credit, and so there's all those factors, but I feel very confident about Model 3 demand. The customer happiness levels with the car is incredible, and I think probably the highest of any car in the world right now, I think. And I think basically nobody wants to sell a car.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: So.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: But the target price point is, I think, in the past, you mentioned mid 40,000. Is that where we're thinking? Or [indiscernible] long-term range?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, this is really just a guess. So it's not like it's some huge crystal ball or something. But at high -- at volume, I would expect, this is totally a guess, to be clear, probably an average of $42,000, probably at that kind of level. I'm not sure -- okay.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: And just as a follow up. Your comments that you expect China to be online by the end of the year, but there's a lot of articles that the battery supplier, you're looking at different battery suppliers. I mean, do you have a battery supplier because it seems kind of close to production it's supposed to start?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Well, there's really 3 things: The cell, the module and the pack. We will be making the module and the pack, so it's really a question of self supply. And because essentially use any high 2170 chemistry and we expect to be a combination of cells produced at our Gigafactory in Nevada and cells produced in Japan and cells produced locally in China, and we feel confident that supply to hit the 3,000 units we need.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Our next question comes from Emmanuel Rosner with Deutsche Bank.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: First, wanted to ask you about the short-range Model 3. What are your latest thoughts in terms of timing of introduction? I think at some point, you had in mind to do it maybe in the first half of this year. And just to clarify, when you're sort of talking about the outlook for 2019, the number of deliveries up 50% and then the margin target for Model 3 to get to 25%, does that assume that you're introducing a lower range, the short-range Model 3 at some point during the year?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Well, if you called the standard range, but it's maybe short by Tesla's standards, but it's long range my other manufacturer standards. But yes, I would expect to introduce the standard range Model 3 sometime probably in the middle of this year is a rough, rough guess. And we're working hard to improve our costs of production, our overhead costs, our fixed cost, just costs generally. I think this past year while extremely difficult, has driven us to a high level of financial discipline. I think we're way smarter about how we spend money and we're getting better with each passing week. Yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: And so to be clear, the -- you expect to reach at some point this year, or you're targeting at some point this year, 25% gross margins on Model 3, and that's despite introducing the lower end -- or just the standard range Model 3, is that correct?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Okay. And I guess my follow up would be on the demand side. So you're talking about 50% increase this year. You said a few times that it could be higher than this. I think you just mentioned in the previous question 350,000 to 500,000, if I understood well. So what is like what sort of drives the cautious outlook that's in your letter? Because it feels like it's the -- it's just basically 4 times the fourth quarter run rate, which would imply sort of 50% for the full year but not really a lot of growth versus what you just accomplished. So I guess how do we think about the total demand for 2019, especially if you introduced this, the cheaper version?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Well, we need to bring the Shanghai factory online, I think that's the biggest driver for getting to the 500K plus a year. Our car is just very expensive going into China. We've got duties, we got transport costs, we've got higher cost lever here, and we've never been eligible for any of the tax credits. A lot of people precisely has been dependent on incentives. In fact, we are -- for talking about EVs, we have the least access incentives. It's pretty crazy because there's so many companies of that -- countries that have put price caps on the EV incentive which so far affect Tesla. And in China, which is the biggest market for EVs, we've had never subsidies or tax incentives for vehicles. So it's -- what's caused me there is eligible for that, but it sounds like that's going to be reducing in China in a coming years. But bottom line is, we need the Shanghai factory to achieve that 10k rate and other cars be affordable. The demand for -- it's appointed appreciate. The demand for Model 3 is insanely high. The inhibitor is affordability. It's just like people literally don't have the money to buy the car. It's got nothing to do with the desire. They just don't have enough money in their bank account. If the car -- if the car would be made more affordable, the demand is extraordinary.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Our next question comes from Pierre Ferragu with New Street Research.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: So Deepak, I was wondering, so as you get to 2019, we're all concerned about the potential recession and I was wondering how you think about it and what you would tell us about what we should expect, how we should expect Tesla to react to recession in 2019? How do you not manage your volume land? How do you manage your pricing? How do you present cash? How do you manage CapEx if things pass in 2019? And then I have a follow up on gross margin?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, it's a very broad question which is not really just for me to answer. But I think, at the highest level, the way we are trying to be prepared for any kind of contingency here is to just continue focusing on cost. And the theme of our conversations here is how to reduce cost all the time and how do we run our business with a very high level of financial discipline, and Elon alluded to that and so did Jerome. I think that if we do that, we believe even in some of those scenarios of lower volumes and pricing type pricing, we do have a good chance and a good shot of being profitable and generate free cash flow. So that's the best way to manage the business, be frugal.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, I hate to be a broken record about this, but its costs, costs, costs because reducing our costs, while making improvements to Model 3, I want to emphasize, the product is getting better by [indiscernible] degrees despite lower costs in hundreds of small ways, but you actually ones you would notice explicitly, but appreciate subconsciously. And getting those costs down variable cost and fixed cost is what allows us to lower the price and be financially sustainable and achieve our mission of environmental sustainability. So we have to be absolute about this. There's no question.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: The other aspect of this, Elon, which is doing extremely well as capital efficiently. We have dramatically cut back on capital expense, and we're spending it in a very efficient manner. We talk about it in a letter on Model 3 and Gigafactory Shanghai. We talked about it for Model Y. There are just so many learnings that we are incorporating, and we just want to do what we did with Model 3 and the kind of spending with the terms we had.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Absolutely. I think like, we're confident that our CapEx per unit of production for Shanghai factory and for Model Y will be less than half of what we did for Model 3. Internally, we think it might be a quarter, but that's probably too good to believe, but it's definitely less than half.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Our next question comes from David Tamberrino with Goldman Sachs.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: First thing I want to just understand is on what you're seeing from European orders and China orders so far. There's some numbers that get thrown around, but you guys are obviously taking a look at it. How is order profile shaping up relative to what you saw in the U.S. with the launch of the 3?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: I think it seems good. I -- our share actually with Europe and China is, how do we get the cars made in and on a boat as such that it reaches the customers before the end of the quarter and we don't have a massive number of cars on the water. That's our biggest challenge. It's not demand. It's how do we get the cars there fast enough.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: So like orders above, I think I've seen like 20,000 order levels for Europe and single-digit thousands for China is better than that, Elon?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, absolutely. The -- I mean, we're not really trying, I should point out. I guess it's -- our factory is like right now only making cars for China and Europe. That's all it's doing for the Model 3. And our hope is how do we get the cars made, get them on the ship as fast as possible, get the ship as fast as possible Belgium, get them over to Grohmann in Norway and get those cars to customers as fast as possible, get them to China's fast as possible. In China, we also -- we don't what's going to happen with the trade negotiations. It's very important, especially in China, as fast as possible. We hope the trade negotiations go well, but it's not clear. But we need to get them there while there's sort of factory, sort of a truce on the tariff war. And the demand is not really one of the things we're thinking about.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Okay. Then just lastly on this demand thread, customer deposit came in again over $100 million. Is it possible to give us an update? I know you don't think it's really a relevant number, but I do know explain why on the reservation count where you were 450,000 you started delivering. And I ask this because I think we're just all trying to understand how much incremental demand you think there is based on what you see at that lower price points if, say, there's over half of those people that are still waiting for that 75k base model to come out, that will be interesting, and I think that's what you're seeing, but I just want to confirm that.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: So Deepak, do you want to?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: I mean I think resolutions are not relevant for us. We are really focused on orders. Now we do have a large reservations backlogs which tells us that a lot of customers are still waiting for those cars, but I don't think it's appropriate to share [indiscernible]

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Our reservations are just like preorders. There's some video game come out and there's a preorder number, then that's like stops being important once you start shipping the gamer product. So as I said earlier, I think my guess is demand is a somewhere order -- in China economies in the order of 700,000 or 800,000 units a year for Model 3 and even in recessions probably in the order of 0.5 million.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Our next question comes from Daniel Ives with Wedbush Securities.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: So my question is around Europe. Obviously, with deliveries coming on board in the first quarter, maybe what surprised you in terms of -- the demand looked strong, but in terms of what you're seeing at the region, is it stronger than you expected in certain countries? What do you think is driving that? And maybe you can just talk about the opportunities and challenges in Europe especially from a delivery with just logistics perspective?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Well, like I said, we're thinking about demand almost 0 right now. It's really getting the product there in time and not having a ton of cars on the water [ ending the quarter ] and then for China getting cars there before there's potential rise in tariffs. That's really pretty way up high. Net cost reduction and then improving service in North America. Yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: And then just maybe a quick follow up on -- can you just talk about, when we look at the Gigafactory build out in China and obviously how important that is, can you just maybe fast forward, let's say, 18, 24 months, I mean, how do you envision that as a competitive advantage versus maybe some other automakers that will be trying to go in your tracks?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: I think it will be quite a significant advantage. It's really fundamental to the future of Tesla, and I expect to make several trips to China this year and I'm working very closely with the team building the factory. I get daily updates on. So it's a similar big deal. And we're really just talking about Phase 1 here. Phase 1 is about 10% of what we think the Gigafactory will ultimately be. So it's a major, major deal. And we're getting a lot of support from the Shanghai government, which we're very appreciative of, and the national government.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Our next question comes from Toni Sacconaghi with Bernstein.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: You've talk repeatedly about the need to drive down costs which, in turn, drives the elasticity of demand for cars. And I'm wondering if you could talk about how much of a price differential between the $50,000 Model 3 and the $35,000 Model 3 is structural; meaning that powertrain costs for EVs are just structurally higher than they are for internal combustion engine cars and where you think that difference is today and when that is no longer a factor. So is -- or maybe said another way, is the bigger driver in getting to lower costs and lower -- and more affordability on the Model 3, is it really around the powertrain and getting that at parity? Or is it everything else about Tesla not being as efficient as other manufacturers that is causing the higher price right now? And have I follow up, please.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: It's both, it's both the vehicle and both the powertrain.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, I split my time half and half between the Gigafactory and here, and there is opportunities in both, yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: But I think the bigger point is, yes, the cost-reduction opportunities are, but the bigger point is it's not that our cost is higher than a gas-powered internal combustion engine.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: I think what Toni meant is with the battery pack, as in battery pack as well as the powertrain together are more expensive than an engine.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: That's true.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: How big do you think that delta is today? And when -- do you think of it as being kind of $10,000, $11,000 for that pack plus powertrain for an electric vehicle and maybe $5,000 or $6,000 for an internal combustion engine car? And is that sort of the order of magnitude? And where do you see those getting much more aligned just sort of given the loss of where you think sell and pack costs are going?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Well, the biggest part to bear in mind is because of electricity, it's probably less than the cost of gasoline, especially in Europe or in California where basically almost everywhere except, say, the middle of the United States, but the cost of gasoline is very expensive and electricity is far cheaper. The -- so that factors into the cost of ownership pretty significantly. It's sort of gone in the order of $50 to $100 a month depending on how much somebody drives. So that's a very important thing to consider for an electric car versus a gasoline car.  That said, in terms of the initial cost of acquisition, I think it's probably -- this is -- this is just off the top of my head, not a calculated number, probably on the order of $7,000 but trending towards $4,000 or $5,000. It's off the top of my head.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Okay. And as you think about 2019, you talked about sort of scenarios for demand and how you plan to roll out the intermediate range and then ultimately the standard range. What is -- if you do have to make a trade-off on volume or profitability during the course of the year, meaning, to get the volume you need, you think you can deliver, you have to go to the lower margins or vice versa, where's the trade-off? Is -- are units produced most important to you? Or is delivering the 25% gross margin more important? So if you have a chance to deliver 450,000 or 500,000 cars but they'll be more standard editions and gross margins will end the year at 20%, is that -- are you willing to make that trade-off?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: My guess is it ends up being sort of about the [indiscernible] together where if given the amount of free cash flow you decide to achieve that with the smaller production or small volume cars and at a higher margin our large volume cars on a smaller margin. I think we're towards the second. We're going to make mark more cars at a lower margin, but I think it's more or less a flat rate.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Our next question comes from Maynard Um with Macquarie.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Can you just update us on where battery costs are now and where you anticipate they'll be by year-end? I'm just trying to gauge how much of a factor this is to lowering costs and sustaining profitability.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: So it's highly proprietary number. We cannot give it out. We'd like to tell you, but no. We do think we have the best costs in the world. The best to our knowledge, our costs are not like anyone else right now and [indiscernible]

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Maybe talk about your expectations with the Panasonic JV and how it might impact you? Is this something that you're made aware of?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: I spoke directly with the [indiscernible] about this, head of Panasonic and he assured me this will have no impact on Tesla.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Our next question comes from Dan Galves with Wolfe Research.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Do you plan to offer U.S. leased product for Model 3 in the U.S? When can we expect it? And can you talk about what percentage of S and X have historically been leased in the U.S.?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Well, we've been reluctant to introduce a lease on Model 3 because its effect on GAAP financials. So it's worth noting the demand to date was your leasing. So obviously losing is a way to improve demand, but it has -- it makes our financial looks worse, but what we wanted to introduce that right away. I mean, we will introduce it sometime later this year probably. I'm not sure what the standard lease on S and X light right now is?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: [indiscernible] 20%, low 20s, that stable at that level for many, many quarters, which is it speaks to an actual demand because we don't do artificially bump up.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, exactly. Obviously [indiscernible] It's usually a small business tax bridle is important for the leasing. So.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: And I have just like 2 quick housekeeping questions. One, is there a restructuring charge that you expect in the first quarter? How much is it? And is it included in your expectation of a small profit?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, it is included in that. It's difficult to say exactly what that is. At this point, I'd say roughly around $40 million, but that number can vary slightly.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Okay. And the last one is...

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Sorry, go ahead.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, the last one is, this change in your service parts structure to make things more distributed rather than in the parts warehouse is, will that be like a meaningful working capital drag? What's the cash impact of that?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: No, it's actually -- we've just been very silly about where we store our parts. So it's actually going to be no change in sort of working capital or not somebody would even notice in the financial. It's just being smarter about sending parts directly to service centers. In fact, either directly from our factory here or from our suppliers and just ship and direct to the service center. Right now our costs will -- our costs will improve, I think, actually quite a lot. Resistance actually quite -- the system is quite grown had actually speaking, self referentially. So it's just being -- so stopping doing the foolish things will massively improve our service costs, will massively improve customer happiness around the world and it's just fundamentally better all around. There are some pretty -- we've been just to Superdome some of the things we've done where like China last year what's are we doing wrong what can we fix unlike her China team is great there like well, you think we can have spare parts that are made in China just sent directly to our China service centers because currently, there's a bunch of parts that are made in China then sent to a warehouse in New Jersey and then sent back to China. Literally what was happening. Super not stop. So it's going to get way better, and yes, it's very clear.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Our next question comes from Ben Kallo with Baird.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: I have 1 question and it's got 4 parts to it. Happy new year, Elon. So the first part is, so Street numbers consensus, we've got everything wrong for 6 years or 7 years public and there's about $6 in earnings. Talk to us about that if you can. Number two, Elon, could you talk to us about, you talk to us about -- I hear you cut some workforce SpaceX and at Tesla. I feel that you have a worry about global economy. Can you talk to us about how you feel about that with your guidance in order -- in the same order? And then can we talk about maybe sort of the third thing for JV. No one's ever going to talk about [indiscernible] but we've got a whole page on that with some pretty good fit, and what should we be focusing on that? And what can that add to the bottom line on top of the $6 this next year?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: I mean, we can't really, Ben, talk about consensus and what that means. I think that -- maybe the better approach is, we are providing certain guidance here, and you and the other analysts need to reflect that in your modeling and that's the best indication from the company of our projections. In all fairness, that's the best way I can think of answering your question here.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, J.B. is there anything?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: I think the letter outlines the predicted growth in the battery storage business, the stationary storage business pretty clearly, and that should be included in the projections as well, so we're excited about it, but I can't say much more detailed.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Our internal projections for stationary storage are closer to 3 gigawatt hours. But some of it is kind of lumpy and may not be completed this year. We would have done more in stationary storage last year, but vehicle production so we had to convert a bunch of stationary storage lines, battery lines, to vehicle battery lines. Otherwise, we would've done quite a bit more in stationary storage. I expect that to grow probably twice as fast as automotive. For sure for a long time.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: We continue to set production records basically every month.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: And the profitability of the storage business and the gross margin continued to improve as we keep ramping up production and scale.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: It's going to be gigantic business down the road.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: And the last question was about economy, global economy.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, sure. I do think that the economy moves in cycles and there's fairly  significant risk of a recession over the next 12 to 18 months, but I'm confident that Tesla will remain at least slightly profitable even with the -- even if there is a significant recession. And then when it will be stronger for us when the recession. And yes, but what we have to be relentless about cost in order to make affordable cars and not go back up. That's what our headcount reduction is about, yes. Yes. It's -- we're super hardcore about it. It's the only way to make affordable cars. On the SpaceX side, cost reduction was for different reasons, unrelated to -- it's basically absolutely insane projects normally bankrupt the company Starship and Starlink, and so SpaceX has to be incredibly spartan with expenditures until those programs reach fruition.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Great. I think that's all the time we have for today. Thank you very much for your questions, and Elon would like to have some closing remarks.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes. So let's see. The -- so Deepak is -- well, make a big announcement, but Deepak is going to be retiring.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Again.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, from Tesla. [indiscernible] [indiscernible] about 11 years ago, right?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, close to that.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, almost 11 years. Thank you for your tremendous contribution to Tesla, and it's his retirement will not be immediate, but Deepak will continue to be at Tesla for a few more months and will continue to serve as senior adviser to Tesla for probably years and years to come hopefully. And we thought long and hard about who the right person is to take over from Deepak, and that's Zach. And Zach has been with Tesla now for 9 years...

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: 9 years.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: So Zach, management work, Wharton undergrad and then worked at Tesla and then's got a couple of years at Harvard Business School, which I actually don't think was necessary, by the way.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: You told me that when I came back.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, exactly. So Zach's incredibly talented, has made a huge contribution to Tesla over the years, and obviously very well known to the whole team and has the respect of the whole team. And Zach, I don't know if you'd like to say a few words?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, I will.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Okay.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Thank you. First of all, Elon, thank you very much for the opportunity for me to be here and be here again a second time. I've learned a lot from you and I've been always inspired by you and I've been also very inspired by the team at Tesla who are incredibly brilliant, very passionate, and just amazingly perseverant, the best team I could imagine. So thank you everybody for that. There is no good time to make this change. We thought it was a good time, it's a new chapter, a new year. Tesla has had 2 great quarters of profitability, cash flow. So now a really solid foundation. And I feel really good about Zach taking over as the CFO. He's proven himself with his many years of experience in many top challenges that he's worked on and really excited to have Zach take on this role, and I'll be here to support him and wish you all successful as a company.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Yes, well, thank you, Deepak. Thank you, Elon. So my name is Zach [indiscernible]. Just a brief background on myself. So I joined Tesla just under 9 years ago when it was super small company with a lot of potential ahead of us, and I was attracted to the mission of the vision of the company. So at that time, I've been deep in the operation of every major program of the company, from the Roadster to Model S and X, Model 3, scaling our Energy business and more things to come which we've talked about on the call. I feel we're starting 2019 with a very strong financial foundation. We have enough cash to continue launching new programs and developing new technologies, and we're able to service upcoming debt obligations with our forecasted cash flows. My focus alongside the talented and amazingly passionate team at Tesla is to ensure we continue the terrific momentum on cost management and operational efficiency which will enable us -- enable more access to our products around the world which is key to achieving our mission as a company. On a personal note, Deepak, a huge thank you to you for your leadership, mentorship and support, and very much looking forward to discussing our progress on future earnings calls.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Great.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Great. Thank you very much. We'll speak to you in 3 months.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Thanks, guys.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q4 2018 Earnings Call, Jan 30, 2019
componenttext: Ladies and gentlemen, thank you for participating in today's conference. This concludes the program. You may all disconnect, and have a wonderful day.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Good day, ladies and gentlemen, and welcome to the Tesla Q1 2019 Financial Results and Q&A Webcast. My name is Sherry, and I will be your coordinator for today. [Operator Instructions] As a reminder, this conference is being recorded. I would now like to turn the call over to your host, Mr. Martin Viecha, Senior Director of Investor Relations. Mr. Viecha, you may now proceed.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext:
Thank you, Sherry, and good afternoon, everyone. Welcome to Tesla's First Quarter 2019 Q&A Webcast. I'm joined today by Elon Musk, J.B. Straubel, Zachary Kirkhorn and a number of other executives. 
Our Q1 results were announced at about 2 p.m. Pacific time in the update letter we published at the same link as this webcast. 
During this call, we will discuss our business outlook and make forward-looking statements. These comments are based on our predictions and expectations as of today. Actual events or results could differ materially due to a number of risks and uncertainties, including those mentioned in our most recent filings with the SEC. [Operator Instructions] 
But before we jump into Q&A, Elon has some opening remarks. Elon?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext:
Thanks, Martin. On Monday, we hosted our first-ever Autonomy Investor Day showcasing our new in-house design full self-driving computer and our AI-based software trained by more than 400,000 Tesla vehicles. All Tesla class today have all hardware necessary for full self-driving and over the year updates will enable our customers to use the Tesla ride-hailing network fleet and generate income which, as we said on Autonomy Day a few days ago, we think is somewhere between $10,000 and $30,000 a year. In some cases, perhaps more. We're the only company in the world producing our own vehicles and batteries as far as our own in-house chip for full self-driving. We're in a position unlike anyone else in the industry. And in 2020, we expect to have 1 million robo taxis on the road with the hardware necessary for full self-driving. We believe we'll have the profitable autonomous taxi on the market and perhaps -- yes.
Last quarter, we experienced a massive increase in delivery volume in Europe, similar to what North America experienced last year as well as the massive increase in delivery volume, too, to China. 
As far as challenges go, this was a good one to have because we booked vehicles and consumers support them this rapid increase in overseas volume [indiscernible] and resulted in over half of our global deliveries occurring in the final 10 days of Q1. 
This was the most difficult logistics problem I've ever seen, and I've seen some tough ones. So I'll say it again, equipment fleet half of all vehicles produced -- half of all deliveries occurred literally in the final 10 days of Q1.
As a result, a large number of vehicles -- vehicle deliveries shifted into Q2 which, of course, Q1 net income to be negatively impacted. As we said, we could not get the vehicles to customers specifically in time. The response to this, we are in the process regionally balancing our vehicle booked throughout the quarter. This will make the -- this will put much strain on Tesla, results in a much better delivery experience for customers and have a very positive effect on our working capital in the middle of the quarter.
In Q1, Model 3 was yet again the best-selling premium car in the U.S., outselling the runner up by almost 60%. It's worth to dwell on that for a moment, just how absurd this is compared to predictions that were made several years ago. There are literally [indiscernible] 0 predictions that this would happen, if you go back to just even 5 or 6 years ago that an electric car would be the best-selling premium car in the U.S. And we believe, over time, we'll be the best-selling premium car throughout the world.
And in fact, in Norway, in March, we set a record for the highest sales of any car period ever. And that'd be something similar in [indiscernible] as well. So this is really an incredible achievement by the Tesla team.
Since the introduction of standard range, a standard range class [indiscernible] 70% of previous Model 3 have actually been non-premium vehicles where people actually pay more for a car than they have ever paid for a car. They never anticipated paying so much for a car, but because they want the Model 3 more than they ever wanted a vehicle, they're willing to pay more to get a Model 3. 
And keep in mind, global expansion for the Model 3 has just begun, and this segment is vastly larger internationally than it is in the U.S. We're continuing to make significant improvements to our vehicle lineup, including updating the Model S and X production line to culminate the next generation of powertrains. So we announced this yesterday and we are now in production with the significantly more balanced powertrains for the Model S and X as well as an upgrade to the suspension system to have active adaptive in the suspension system and to enable charging at 200 kilowatts, which is -- and there are number of other small changes. If anyone is thinking about upgrading their Model S or X, this is a great time to do it. And we also introduced a loyalty program where if somebody has an existing Tesla owner and they buy a performance model S or X, they get their first upgrade for free. So this is -- yes, as a thank you and an appreciation to existing Tesla customers.
So they have a longer range. The Model S now has a range of 370 miles. This is actually a range of 370 miles, and Motor Trend test drove a car a few days ago and drove nonstop all the way from San Francisco to Los Angeles at normal highway speeds. And they said they could have even gone faster. And they were any headwind as well. So this is pretty remarkable that an electric car could go nonstop between the 2 biggest cities in California. I mean, I've never -- back when I was driving gas main car, I always have to stop at a gas station. This is literally better than a gasoline car with the rare exception. And there's also an increase in power as it is always faster, just better in every way.
And we're able to increasing the size of the battery pack, which is a testament to the powertrain team and for us to be able to improve the efficiency of the powertrain by such a significant margin.
So we recently announced product improvements on Model S and X as well as continued expansion of Model 3 globally. We expect the order rate to increase significantly throughout the year and increment it with our production levels. 
And I'm very excited about the future for product parts, especially for full Self-Driving, which will finally transform transport as we know it. The Tesla semi truck Model Y, improvements to Powerwall, Powerpack, the Solar Roof version 3 on the energy side. And no question in my mind that Tesla has the most exciting product roadmap of any considered product company in the world . 
And finally, I would like to thank our employees for their incredible work and our customers for their continued support.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Thank you very much, Elon. And I think Zach -- Zachary would like to have some remarks as well.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext:
Yes, thank you, Martin, and thanks, Elon, as well. Overall as we reflect on the progress of Q1, this is one of the most complicated quarters that I can think of in the history of the company. And it was ambitious even by Tesla's standard. The global expansion of Model 3 was a huge theme within the quarter. We launched the standard range lineup for Model 3, product retooling from Model S and Model X, which Elon just talk about, and suspension upgrade and then we implemented various pricing adjustments and worked to do the corresponding impact it had on our order mix on deliverable cars. 
So there's 2 key themes that I'd like to discuss briefly and then we'll open it up to Q&A around cash and profitability for the quarter. 
First, on the cash front, we exited Q1 with $2.2 billion in cash and cash equivalents on hand. This was a $1.5 billion reduction from our 2018 ending cash balance, but this reduction is attributed to 2 factors. The first is that we paid off $920 million convertible note on March 1. 
Note for those of you looking to the cash flow statement, $188 million of this is flowing through to our operating cash flows.  The balance to the $1.5 billion reduction is more than explained by the working capital impact of expanding Model 3 operations overseas. And the 2 components to this which we have discussed, is that an international operation naturally commands additional working capital because of transit times, but then also the stress on our delivery operations meant that not all of are cars will be delivered. 
Both of these factors which occurred in Q1. We do not expect repeat in Q2. And we expect our quarter ending cash balance to continue to increase going forward. I'll also note that we're tracking in April to the largest amount of deliveries for month 1 in the history of the company.
On the working capital point, as Elon noted, 50% of our deliveries in Q1 occurred in the final 10 days of the quarter. This is because we prioritized international build for the first half of the quarter and then last local build in the second half. This led to a binary inflow of Model 3 cars to EMEA and China and significantly stressed the delivery operation. But we also faced important issues in Shanghai and Beijing and worked through those, but that also skewed deliveries towards the final couple of days and weeks in the quarter. So we're addressing this by regionally balanced -- by building regionally balanced, and we've already executed on this for Model 3 and S and X will be implemented in the next week or 2. 
The secondary benefit of this is that it enables us to run stable operations throughout the quarter. So we don't have to staff many of our delivery areas and we'll just stick operation to the fleet. We expect significant cost savings to come from this.
On the P&L side, we incurred $188 million of one-time adjustment that's led through to net income. $120 million of this is related to S and X pricing adjustments that we announced on February 28. This included a reserve for a potential increase for [indiscernible] for a residual value guarantee and buyback guarantee of vehicles and also an adjustment for the inventory value of our used Tesla inventory and service deliveries. There's an additional $67 million related to Q1 restructuring and other charges that flowed through.
Within the automotive business, one thing that I want to note here is that automotive revenue was negatively impacted by $501 million attributed to the reserve increase for S and X that I just noted. If you adjust for this, the decline from Q4 to Q1 in revenue is roughly in line with the decline in deliveries. 
Within automotive gross margin, Model 3 gross margin declined slightly to approximately [ 28% ] to 20%. This is due to 2 factors. One is the pricing adjustments that we made on February 28 as well as makeshift towards the standard range lineup which we launched. We also successfully executed on a number of cost reductions which offset this impact. Labor content warehousing [indiscernible] double-digit improvements from Q4 to Q1. 
In spite of launching the standard range variance, we want to note that North American ASPs are close to $50,000, with the majority of our orders being from long-range bearings of Model 3. 
In S and X, the impact on margin was more significant. 2 major pieces here is the volume reduction led to a reduction in fixed cost absorption so that impacted our margin as well as the pricing actions that we took on February 28. Even though S and X had been in production for a while, we still continue to make operational improvements there, the labor content as an example, which improved quarter-over-quarter.
As we look to the future here, I agree with Elon's sentiments about the excitement of our product line up. But from a financial standpoint, what we've effectively done here is build an incredible base of knowledge and assets that we can quickly scale and replicate into different products around the world. 
So Gigafactory Shanghai is terrific example of this. As we noted in the letter, CapEx [ bringing up ] capacity 50% for Giga Shanghai as compared to the Model 3 in the U.S. and then 50% our internal forecast that we're executing against is actually better than that. 
In Model Y, as we've noted, is built on the Model 3 platform, so we're able leverage the knowledge there for capital efficient. 
In energy as well, as we've noted previously, 2019 is a big year for storage so a lot of exciting tech improvements coming there. And the expansion will help improve margin as we can better utilize some of the assets that we plan to invest in. 
So just to conclude the opening remarks here, I personally never felt more excited about the future of the company, and I'm looking forward to the discussion.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Thank you very much, Zachary. Let's take some first questions from retail shareholders who have been submitting their questions on say.com.  So the first question is, will Tesla be able to complete their purchase of Maxwell Technologies? What is holding that back?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019

componenttext: Thank you. componenttext: The answer is, yes, we are creating a Tesla insurance product, and we hope to launch that in about a month. It will be much more compelling than anything else out there.

Can you speak to the energy side of the company specifically, the roadmap for when you see the energy side of things really taking off and generating major revenue for the company?

componenttext: Sure. The challenge really is battery cells scarcity. As far as the additional storage is concerned, we basically need an upsell to support the vehicle production as well as to full power on Powerpack. Last year, in order to have enough cell for Model 3, we actually had to convert all of the lines of the Gigafactory to produce cells just for Model 3 as opposed to Powerpacks were essentially scrounging cells from all over the world to continue some level of production on the Powerpack. This year, we think that we'll be able to allocate at least maybe 5% to 10% of cell outputs current, J.B. , like what do you guys think?

componenttext: Yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Yes, something like that.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Between 5% and 10%, something like that.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Yes. So there are [indiscernible] cell in Powerwall connected to the car so that translates to a -- quite a decent number of Powerwalls. And then we will continue to use cells from various suppliers around the world. The Powerwall Powerpack, because I don't have to go through vehicle [indiscernible] are much more adaptable to using a variety of cells from other cell providers. So we expect that Powerwall and Powerpack to see a very significant percentage growth this year in maybe the order of 300% or some quite high number. Sorry? Sorry. Yes, 300. Confirming it, 300%. So this is a very big growth rate. It's much faster than an automotive. So over time, we expect that to growth rate will help [indiscernible] and then battery storage will become a bigger and bigger percentage of Tesla's business over time. We also have -- we're also finding a significant increase in retrofit solar this year because we've finally refined the product offering to be something that's extremely compelling and much more cost efficient to deliver and install. So we dramatically streamline the process from what has been done before, and we'll have more to say on that possibly next week. And then the Solar Roof tile, we're on version 3 of the design. That necessarily takes a while to scale up because we have to be confident of the Solar Roof is going to last for -- on the order of 30 years and because the warranty is sort of 20, 25 years. And so the rate at which you can iterate on Solar Roof is necessarily slowed down by the [indiscernible] which you can do accelerated aging on the roof. And we want the installation process to be simple and easy. So which is actually the bulk of Tesla Buffalo factory a few weeks ago and I was pretty impressed with the team and we're looking forward to scaling that up significantly through the balance of this year and next.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: And perhaps the a last question from retail. How soon should current owners purchase FSB, get the new FSB computer?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: I think from a full features and functionality standpoint, I think there's no point getting the FSD upgrade if you don't really have it in the car for probably about 2 or 3 months. That's when we'll start releasing features that are really different from the future set available on the version 2 hardware. So just no need to rush to the -- get your computer replaced. It's like 2 to 3 months before it becomes relevant. And then it will obviously increase rapidly from then. One other comment I'll make in case nobody asked this exclusive especially. For Model Y production, we are right now trying to decide whether Model Y vehicle production should be in California or Nevada, and we expect to make a final decision on that very soon. But in the meantime, we have ordered all of the tooling and equipment required for Model Y. So we don't expect this in any way to delay production of Model Y, but it's hardly a very close call between Nevada and California as to whether we do the Model Y at Giga or at Fremont. But those are the 2 options, and hopefully, we'll be able to make the decision in the next few weeks.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Thank you very much. Sherry, we can go to analyst questions in the question queue.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Our first question comes from Ryan Brinkman with JPMorgan.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Your guidance for 90,000 to 100,000 2Q deliveries when combined with the full year outlook, it suggests somewhere between 35% and 45% sequential growth from the first half to the second. Can you talk about what is giving you the confidence to project that growth? And in particular what the order book or reservation list may be telling you?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Yes. The -- we do see strong demand for vehicles, the -- both S, X and 3. The standard range plus Model 3 with water pot included at $39,500 is just an incredibly compelling vehicle and affordable to probably something in the order of like the top 40% income earners in the U.S. and Europe. So it's -- I think we'll see a lot of interest and demand in that. We are. And then with the upgraded S and X, I think a lot of people were kind of anticipating that there would be an S, X upgrade, and this really is kind of a game changer of an upgrade. So I think we are seeing an uptick in demand, and we see expect that to be quite significant. So -- and we're out of the seasonality of Q1 with few people just don't like buying cars in winter and we're getting past the overhang of the [indiscernible] with which for us ended in the U.S. on December 31. So these were quite positive factors. We also have a lot of markets where there's tapped into demand, especially for Model 3. So we'll be really saying the right-hand drive Model 3 and expect to see significant demand in the right-hand drive countries. Overall, I feel really good about where things are headed.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Okay. Then my follow up. Sorry. Because in the previous call, you indicated that the Y would not be built in Fremont because it was, I think, you said packed to the gills. I heard today that it is now close call between California and Nevada. Is anticipated demand for Fremont built vehicles less than was previously thought? Or have you managed to maybe find more in capacity in Fremont, for example, with the tender some other production method?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Well, first of all, I actually on account of tents, like I mean, we like hard-core tents, not like Cub Scout tents, which are fine, but this is actually -- credit goes to Tesla team because actually look at how can we do this in Fremont if we had to and we feel that we can actually append building space to the -- stick to the west side of the building and use a lot of internal space that's currently used for warehousing in the Fremont factory. And so we believe it actually can be done with minimal disruption to add Model Y to Fremont.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019

componenttext: I think we expect demand to -- we are seeing demand returning to normal in Q2. And it might be a little better than normal. It's -- I don't have a crystal ball, it's hard for me to say, but my impression right now is that demand is quite solid, quite strong, yes.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: And then my second question was briefly on...

[indiscernible] already increasing.

How much is the price points coming down in the mix? And how much is related to pricing and other things?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Sure. So quite a bit, but we think if we didn't unwind or pulled away where we, yes, made cars in the first half of the quarter almost exclusively for Asia and Europe, and the second half almost, exclusively for North America. And then actually even that is subdivided depending upon whether its West Coast or East Coast, then we could deliver more cars. But we think it is important to unwind this wave because it ends up being sort of optimizing for 1 quarter, but really adding a lot of cost and difficulty and not just -- not being a good experience for customers and pretty [indiscernible] of the Tesla team. So if we fully optimize for profitability in Q2, I think we could do it, but then we would be unable to unwind this crazy wave of deliveries. And it also helps on working capital within the quarter to not have the wave. And then, Zach, do you want to talk about some of the other items?

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: Yes. No, I think you summarized it well, Elon. Two other things I would add. One is we did make pricing adjustments to our products in Q1, which puts pressure on margin. And so that's part of what we will see in Q2. The teams are working extremely hard and making progress in improving the cause efficiency of the business without sacrificing growth. And that, in combination with the efficiencies from unwinding the wave, is where we feel we'll be comfortable returning to a place of profitability in Q3 once all of those pieces are in place.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
componenttext: And also, not only 3.5% of our trade-ins for Model 3 are coming from Model S. So it's from all the Model 3 trade-ins, Model S accounts for a super, super tiny portion.

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019

headline: Tesla, Inc., Q1 2019 Earnings Call, Apr 24, 2019
